Our work highlights the often overlooked role of stochasticity in human planning and its impact on planning strategy and effort. Moreover, it reveals the limitations of studying stochasticity solely through single-shot decisions.

articles.title: Separable Cognitive Mechanisms for Item-based and Generalized Learning of Novel Speech
articles.authors: Samuel Weiss-Cowie; Matthew H. Davis
articles.abstract: Perceptual learning and word learning are two processes critical for comprehending novel forms of spoken language, allowing listeners to accommodate unfamiliar speech patterns (e.g., accents) or unknown lexical items (i.e., unfamiliar words), respectively. Here we explore listeners’ abilities to correctly apply each learning process when both are potentially task-relevant. In two online studies, we use semantically constraining sentence contexts to guide participants towards either inferring a pseudoword’s meaning (i.e., word learning; “Every morning, Sophie drank a cup of… /pəbuːf/”) or correcting an accented familiar word’s surface form (i.e., perceptual learning; “The sailors found which way was north using a… /kʌmpəf/”). Study 1 demonstrated that participants can engage both learning processes in a single task. However, the extent of learning depends on the relative number of unique trained items (type frequency) and item repetitions (token frequency). Study 2 showed that suitable type and token frequencies result in robust perceptual and word learning that persists for at least 12 hours. Study 2 further demonstrated that perceptual learning generalizes to recently learned pseudowords following a 12-hour delay and that an individual’s success at one type of learning is uncorrelated with success at the other. The present results show how perceptual learning and word learning can be studied together, yet are observably distinct cognitive processes underpinned by dissociable mechanisms that benefit from different training conditions and are differentially engaged in individual participants. The unique requirements of perceptual and word learning should be carefully considered when designing experiments in which one or other form of learning is desired.

articles.title: United in Wonder: Shared Awe Is Associated With Satisfaction and Depth in Romantic Relationships
articles.authors: Matteo Perini; Lou Mier; Jasper H. B. de Groot; Hein van Schie; Johan Karremans
articles.abstract: Shared awe, a co-experience of vastness and meaning, may strengthen romantic bonds beyond individual awe. Across two preregistered cross-sectional studies of partnered adults (Study 1, N = 198; Study 2, N = 306), participants reported shared awe with their partner, dispositional awe, relationship satisfaction, and self–other overlap; Study 2 additionally assessed willingness to sacrifice, ego decentering, relationship flourishing (a measure of relationship depth), and shared amusement. Shared awe predicted relationship satisfaction and explained unique variance beyond dispositional awe (both studies) and shared amusement (Study 2). Structural equation modeling in Study 2 suggested that the shared awe–satisfaction association was primarily accounted for by indirect pathways via self–other overlap, willingness to sacrifice, and especially relationship flourishing. Together, these results identify shared awe as a distinct relational experience that may deepen romantic bonds and enhance well-being. We discuss these findings within the broader literature on positive experiences in romantic relationships.

articles.title: Two-Year-Olds Reason About Multiple Mutually Exclusive Possibilities
articles.authors: Urvi Maheshwari; Caren Walker; David Barner
articles.abstract: A prominent hypothesis in the literature on logical reasoning suggests that, unlike human adults and older children, 3-year-olds deploy “minimal representation of possibility” when reasoning about the existence of a reward in one of two possible locations. On this view, children are unable to represent two mutually exclusive possibilities simultaneously, and instead commit to one location for the reward in a pair of possible locations, only considering the second location once they have evidence that their first choice is not correct. Although this hypothesis may explain children’s struggles on various tasks of modal reasoning, the idea that children may represent possible reward locations sequentially (rather than simultaneously) has not been directly tested. In the present study, we tested the minimal representations hypothesis by asking 24- to 38-month-olds to bet on two of three possible reward locations, one of which was impossible. In two experiments, we found that children were able to simultaneously identify the two possible locations, while successfully avoiding the impossible location. We conclude that young children’s behaviors reflect reasoning about mutually exclusive possibilities, rather than sequential minimal representations.

articles.title: Computational mechanisms of cocaine use disorder: Inefficient evidence accumulation is associated with trait impulsivity and high frequency cocaine use
articles.authors: Alison Schreiber; Thomas P. Shellenberg; Sean Regnier; David H. Cox; Alexandra N. Johansen; Michael John Wesley; Joshua A. Lile; William Stoops
articles.abstract: High frequency cocaine use is associated with poor psychological and health outcomes. However, we know little about the computational processes that contribute to high frequency use, especially among individuals with cocaine use disorder. Here, we demonstrate a role for efficiency of evidence accumulation (EEA) in these use patterns. Treatment-seeking adults (N = 127) with cocaine use disorder completed cognitive tasks and self-report measures at baseline, as part of a larger trial. Using hierarchical drift diffusion modeling, we estimated subject-level, condition-specific drift rates – the speed of evidence accumulation for goal-relevant stimuli – for two neurocognitive tasks (n-Back, Cued Go/No Go). Confirmatory factor analysis supported the existence of a task-general EEA factor that drives performance across cognitive tasks. Lower EEA statistically mediated the effect of trait impulsivity on high frequency cocaine use. These findings support the view that EEA constitutes a proximal neurocomputational mechanism of high frequency cocaine use.

articles.title: Kode Papua: An Omni-Communal Sign Language of Western New Guinea
articles.authors: David Gil
articles.abstract: This paper introduces Kode Papua, a hitherto undescribed sign language, whose most remarkable feature is that it is used not only by deaf individuals but also by most or all hearing people across the western half of the island of New Guinea.  The paper begins with an account of the settings in which Kode Papua is used, and then provides a brief sketch of selected lexical, grammatical and phonological properties, with reference to a short naturalistic text.  Following this, the paper presents the results of a large-scale comprehension experiment showing that (i) sociolinguistically, Kode Papua is understood equally well by hearing people regardless of whether they are in regular contact with a deaf person, and (ii) geographically, it is limited in its range to New Guinea, with comprehension dropping off substantially in other parts of Indonesia, and even further in more distant locations.  Next, the paper situates Kode Papua in typological space, comparing it to other systems of communication in the visual modality — co-speech gestures, urban sign languages, village sign languages, homesign, and others — with reference to 18 features of a sociolinguistic and structural nature.  Compared to these other communicative systems, Kode Papua displays a unique constellation of properties leading to its characterization as an omni-communal sign language.  Nevertheless, Kode Papua is but a more extreme case of a phenomenon that is common in non-WEIRD (Western, Educated, Industrial, Rich, Democratic) societies of the Global South, namely, the use of sign languages by large proportions of the general hearing population.  The paper concludes with some diachronic and phylogenetic reflections, suggesting that Kode Papua may have a history stretching back at least centuries, and that, more generally, the widespread use of sign languages amongst hearing populations may be reconstructed to an earlier stage in the evolution of the human language landscape.

articles.title: Accounting for age-related differences in the task-evoked pupil response
articles.authors: Michael Andrew Johns; Regina Calloway; I. M. Dushyanthi Karunathilake; Lien P. Decruy; Samira Anderson; Jonathan Z. Simon; Stefanie E. Kuchinsky
articles.abstract: The relationship between pupil size, task performance, and self-reported effort has served as a fruitful way of understanding how listeners prepare (baseline or tonic pupil size) and deploy (task-evoked or phasic pupil response) cognitive resources during a listening task. However, changes in pupil size are sensitive not only to changes in listening effort but also physiological differences, some of which may be confounded across groups. Age is one such factor: older adults’ pupil size and dynamic range tend to diminish with age (senile miosis), obscuring researchers’ ability to compare how older and younger adults prepare and deploy listening effort. To account for this age-related physiological discrepancy in pupil size, methods to normalize or scale pupil size have been proposed. Whether these methods account for physiological differences while preserving potential task-evoked differences has not yet been evaluated. The present study seeks to address this by comparing three scaling methods (baseline subtraction, luminance dynamic range scaling, and task dynamic range scaling), as well as showing the pitfalls of performing no scaling at all. To accomplish this, pupil size was recorded while normal-hearing younger and older adults listened to 60-second storybook passages in quiet. The interaction between baseline pupil size and the task-evoked pupil response was analyzed using generalized additive mixed models. The results showed that applying no scaling at all or only performing baseline subtraction resulted in significantly smaller pupil responses in older compared to younger adults, while (luminance or task) dynamic range scaling was able to account for physiological differences between groups while simultaneously capturing the dynamic relationship between baseline pupil size and the task-evoked pupil response. Overall, the results of this study suggest that whether and how to apply within-participant scaling depends on the theoretical question to be addressed and the specifics of the study design and analyses.

articles.title: Culture by Design : A Sociotechnical Framework for Culturally Grounded AI for Mental Health
articles.authors: Sunny Rai; Elizabeth Cameron Stade; Rui Zhou; Neil Sehgal; Simone H. Schriger; Sara Gerke; Lyle H Ungar; Sharath Chandra Guntuku
articles.abstract: AI systems for mental health are developed predominantly using data from Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) populations, raising concerns about their validity, fairness, and generalizability across diverse cultural and geographic contexts. This limitation is especially consequential in mental health, where linguistic expression, symptom presentation, help-seeking behavior, and access to care vary substantially across populations. We argue that culture must be treated as a first-class design requirement throughout the AI development lifecycle, not an afterthought applied post-hoc, a principle we term culture by design. Drawing on evidence from NLP, clinical psychology, HCI, and global mental health, we present ten practical recommendations spanning data collection, modeling, evaluation, deployment, and governance, providing researchers and practitioners with a concrete roadmap for building culturally grounded, equitable, and contextually appropriate mental health AI systems.

articles.title: Dual representation of self and tutor song in basal ganglia neurons during vocal learning
articles.authors: Yuka Y. Suzuki; Hiroki Koda; Kazuo Okanoya; Shin Yanagihara
articles.abstract: Imitation learning depends on neural mechanisms that transform sensory targets into the motor commands needed to reproduce them, but the specific neural substrates underlying this transformation remain unclear. Songbirds provide a powerful model for studying neural mechanisms of vocal imitation because juveniles learn to sing by imitating a tutor, and auditory–vocal mirror neurons in the premotor nucleus respond both during singing and during playback of the bird’s own song. Here, using single-unit recordings, we identified auditory–vocal mirror neurons in the song basal ganglia (sBG), a major downstream target of the premotor nucleus within the song learning circuit. These neurons fired both during singing and during playback of the bird’s own song in both adult and juvenile Bengalese finches. Notably, during the sensorimotor learning period of juveniles, auditory responses of these neurons were related not only to the bird’s own song but also to the tutor song, the sensory target for imitation. These findings extend auditory–vocal mirror coding beyond the premotor nucleus to the basal ganglia and suggest that the dual representation of self-generated song and tutor song in sBG neurons may support the sensory–motor processes underlying vocal imitation learning.

articles.title: International vaping and nicotine cessation services: A desk review
articles.authors: Rory Coyne; Mengyang Zhang; Gintare Valentelyte; Paul Kavanagh; Jan Sørensen; Frank Doyle
articles.abstract: Existing services for vaping cessation are sparse, while the rise of alternative nicotine products such as pouches - which are placed between the gum and lip for potent nicotine delivery - will also require effective and accessible support for those who wish to quit. In Ireland, the Health Service Executive does not currently have a model of care for the treatment of addiction to these products. The aim of this desk review was to engage with international service providers involved in nicotine cessation, to gain insights into the services currently available to users of vapes and/or nicotine products like pouches. A desk review was undertaken through a series of semi-structured interviews with international service providers (N = 8) of nicotine and tobacco cessation services. Questions centred around current approaches to managing vaping and alternative nicotine products, types of products, patterns of use, service user cohorts, current challenges, and future recommendations. Overall, many of the providers discussed a move in their cessation services from focusing on individual products towards an integrated focus on nicotine dependence, and recommend offering tailored support to specific at-risk cohorts, particularly adolescents and young adults. Providers highlighted challenges related to engagement, such as low acceptability and difficulties in self-monitoring vape use patterns. Social and environmental risks were also reported, such as unsafe sourcing of vapes and pouches (e.g., scavenging) and health harms associated with pouches. They also identified several effective strategies which were successful in promoting adherence as well as successfully quitting – they included adopting a motivational, non-judgemental ethos, using practical substitutions for the flavoured and sensory components of vaping, and offering sustained support. Providers also discussed several emerging challenges and shared recommendations for developing and refining future services.

articles.title: What determines the success of AI voice-cloned speech? Prosodic and acoustic evidence on three TTS systems
articles.authors: Linda Bakkouche; Xinbing Luo; Emily Lau; Charles Mcghee; Stephanie Cooper; Brechtje Post; Kai Alter; Julia Schwarz
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) voice cloning systems have advanced rapidly, enabling applications in education and assistive technologies. Yet listeners’ perceptual ratings of naturalness and similarity remain inconsistent: some systems approach human-like quality, while others sound noticeably artificial. Here we present a comprehensive prosodic and computational analysis of voice-cloned speech across three voice-cloning systems (ElevenLabs, StyleTTS-V2, XTTS-V2), building on the listener judgments of these stimuli reported in Bakkouche et al. (2025). We analysed pitch, amplitude, speech rate, rhythm, intonation, and speaker-embedding similarity. Overall, ElevenLabs showed the closest correspondence to human speech across several prosodic and speaker-identity measures, although system differences were not uniform across all dimensions. The clearest acoustic differences between models were observed in speech rate, vowel-based rhythm measures, local pitch-control measures, and speaker-embedding similarity. These acoustic findings are consistent with listeners’ perceptual judgments of naturalness and suggest that prosodic timing, rhythm, and fine-grained pitch control are potential correlates of perceived naturalness, and that improvement of these features can contribute to the development of more natural-sounding synthesised speech.

articles.title: Beyond minimal effects: a cumulative digital exposure framework for digital childhood
articles.authors: Velislava Hillman; Yujeong Hwang; Louise Holly; Rebecca Foljambe; Mandy Pierlejewski; Sara Hawley; Daniel Wisniewski
articles.abstract: Digital technologies are increasingly embedded within the infrastructures of childhood as they are now shaping learning and everyday developmental environments. However, research on digital technology use and well-being has largely relied on time-based exposure measures that consistently report small associations with global well-being indicators. This conceptual review synthesises interdisciplinary evidence from behavioural science, epidemiology, clinical research and educational studies to examine the limitations of prevailing exposure models and to reconceptualise digitally mediated childhood as a cumulative exposure process. Drawing on these literatures, the article develops a cumulative digital exposure framework that conceptualises digital engagement as a multi-level system in which algorithmic platform design, institutional embedding and developmental processes interact over time. The review highlights how time-based and snapshot measurement approaches may be poorly suited to capturing adaptive, personalised and cumulative exposure dynamics characteristic of contemporary digital ecosystems. Evidence across clinical, educational and public health domains suggests that digital environments may influence development through cumulative effects on attention, emotional regulation, learning practices and physical health. By reframing digital technology use as a cumulative and system-level exposure, this review contributes a theoretical foundation for advancing research on digital childhood and outlines implications for measurement, causal inference and population health approaches in digital health research.

articles.title: Beauty Within Spirituality: An Investigation Into the Aesthetic Judgement of Photographs Among Japanese Adults
articles.authors: Yizhen Zhou; Hideaki Kawabata
articles.abstract: Spirituality comprises transformative experiences linked to self-transcendence and perceived connections with a higher being. Although previous research suggests that aesthetic experiences can be transformative and that spirituality has long been associated with the arts, empirical investigation of their relationship is nascent. Moreover, empirical aesthetics has predominantly relied on Western-centric samples, overlooking aesthetic variations across populations. To examine the concept of spirituality within the domain of aesthetic experiences, particularly among a relatively underrepresented population, this study assessed the relationship between spirituality and aesthetic evaluations of photographs among Japanese adults, while incorporating nostalgia as a related emotional dimension. Results from a large, age-diverse cohort indicate a positive correlation between beauty ratings of photographs and perceived spirituality, but no direct association between spirituality and nostalgia ratings. Individuals with higher trait-level spirituality or nostalgia rated photographs as more beautiful, and trait spirituality was associated with stronger spirituality and nostalgia ratings. These findings extend current understanding of spirituality in aesthetic contexts among a non-Western population and provide a basis for future cross-cultural research on the mechanisms underlying subjective aesthetic experience.

articles.title: Evaluating Large Language Models for Feature Extraction from Verbal Stimuli: A Simulation-Based Workflow
articles.authors: Tim Angelike; Daniel W. Heck
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as research tools to facilitate the fast and automated extraction of text features. In psychological studies, they have been used to quantify the degree to which verbal stimulus materials reflect certain psychological constructs. However, the application of LLMs entails a high degree of flexibility regarding prompt design (e.g., instruction details and examples) and model specification (e.g., model family, size, and configuration), which can produce divergent results and threaten the robustness and generalizability of conclusions. To navigate the multiverse of possible choices, we develop a structured workflow for evaluating the quality of LLM feature extraction across diverse model and prompt specifications. Motivated by generalizability theory, the workflow distinguishes between construct variance across stimulus items, method variance due to model and prompt choices,  and error variance across repeated iterations. To guide researchers through the planning, execution, and reporting of LLM simulation studies, we introduce an adapted version of the ADEMP template (Aims, Data-generating mechanism, Estimands and targets, Methods, Performance measures), originally developed for methodological simulation research. The template supports two complementary validation strategies: variance decomposition for studying consistency across LLM specifications and external validation against human gold-standard ratings. In a pre-registered case study using locally runnable, open-weight LLMs, we illustrate the workflow by examining the influence of model choice, response format, and prompt examples on the quality of valence and arousal ratings for multi-word expressions. We additionally assess the efficacy of aggregating repeated, stochastic LLM ratings to improve feature extraction quality.

articles.title: We may know what we will remember: An investigation of visual memory for paintings
articles.authors: Yizhen Zhou; Hideaki Kawabata
articles.abstract: Previous research on visual memory has indicated that memorability is an intrinsic property of images; furthermore, predictions of memorability generated by computational models generally outperform human subjective judgments. However, most studies have used photographs to examine the correlations between memory judgment and visual performance; limited studies have used other visual stimuli, such as paintings. Moreover, how semantic categories of paintings affect their memorability remains relatively unclear. Therefore, this study investigated the influence of categories on subjective memorability judgments and actual memory performance using paintings ranging from abstract paintings to individual portraits, categorized as a continuum based on biological factors. Contrary to previous studies that used naturalistic photographs, our results demonstrated that human subjective judgments correlated more robustly and consistently with actual memory performance than with predictions generated by a neural network model. Furthermore, although a significant overall effect of categories on memory performance was observed, pairwise comparisons between the categories were not significant. Thus, the effect of categories may not be driven by a rigid hierarchical contrast in semantic categorization. These findings suggest that semantic categories and presentation medium may influence visual memorability. Furthermore, they may encourage research aiming to validate the advantages of human metacognition and elucidate the mechanisms underlying this phenomenon when evaluating whether an artwork is memorable.

articles.title: Phased development of REAL-TALK (Representative Elicitation and Analysis of Language - Toolkit of Applied Language tasKs): Toward a Realistic and Person-Centered Monologic Discourse Assessment Battery and Analysis Tool for People with Acquired Language Impairment
articles.authors: Brielle C Stark; Emma Williams; Alana Woods; Sarah Grace Dalton
articles.abstract: Current monologic discourse tasks used in aphasia assessment often rely on outdated, decontextualized materials that do not adequately reflect the everyday communication demands, identities, or priorities of people with aphasia (PWA). In this pre-implementation design paper, we describe the phased development of REAL-TALK: the Representative Elicitation and Analysis of Language – Toolkit of Applied Language tasKs. REAL-TALK is a new discourse elicitation battery designed to support person-centered care across the continuum of aphasia recovery through co-designed stimuli and procedures, graded task complexity, optional supports, and digital deployment. Guided by the Exploration, Preparation, Implementation, Sustainment (EPIS) framework, the present work is positioned within the Exploration and Preparation stages. Phase I included two virtual focus groups and a follow-up confirmatory survey with nine adults with chronic post-stroke aphasia. Participants called for discourse tasks that are contemporary, colorful, culturally diverse, personally meaningful, clearly explained, and tiered by difficulty. They emphasized the importance of topic choice, clinician-guided personalization, feedback, reflection over time, and stronger connections to everyday communication. Across genres, participants favored inference-based picture description prompts, real-world picture sequences, personal narratives, and procedural tasks linked to activities of daily living, while traditional story-retell tasks such as Cinderella were generally viewed as outdated, overly complex, and less motivating. These findings provide a community-informed blueprint for Preparation-stage development of REAL-TALK, including candidate stimulus creation, iterative review with PWA and speech-language pathologists, within-genre difficulty tiering, and later clinical validation.

articles.title: From Foundations to Frontiers: Fit-for-Purpose Discourse Science
articles.authors: Brielle C Stark
articles.abstract:
Purpose: This clinical focus piece responds to discussion at the 2026 International Cognitive Communication Disorders Conference (ICCDC), where participants highlighted a productive tension that has shaped discourse research for decades. Canonical discourse elicitation tasks (e.g., Cinderella retell) remain valuable because they support historical continuity, cross-study comparison, and shared datasets, yet they do not always capture the discourse constructs most relevant for all populations or clinical purposes. Discourse analysis has long been central to the assessment and treatment of neurogenic and cognitive-communication communication disorders, and canonical tasks have shaped decades of research by yielding replicable, analyzable, and linguistically rich language samples.
Method: This clinical focus article is a narrative review that draws on the author’s personal experience as a translational researcher and deep knowledge of the field.
Results and Conclusion: This clinical focus piece examines what canonical tasks revealed about language breakdown, why they succeeded within their original scope, where they may be inappropriate, and why and when they should still be retained. Building on discussion from ICCDC, this piece argues that the field does not simply need “new tasks,” but rather a principled framework for deciding when canonical tasks remain useful, when they should be adapted, and when new tasks, methods, and metrics are warranted. A fit-for-purpose discourse science is proposed, in which discourse tasks and outcome measures are selected because they are appropriate, robust, and aligned with the purpose, population, and construct of interest, while also integrating psychometric rigor, participatory design, and thoughtful use of technologies such as natural language processing and perceptual ratings. This clinical focus piece also offers practical tables summarizing elicitation methods, suitable measures, key features of strong discourse assessment, clinically feasible implementation principles, and example scenarios for choosing canonical and non-canonical tasks in research and practice.

articles.title: SAS-1: A single-item version of the Smartphone Addiction Scale
articles.authors: Jay A. Olson; Ellen Langer; Loren Martin
articles.abstract: The digital age has brought increasing concern about the negative effects of smartphones, which has led to dozens of measures of problematic smartphone use. Almost all of these measures, however, may be too long for contexts such as large-scale surveys. Here, we introduce a single-item measure that probes agreement with the statement: ‘I am addicted to my smartphone’. Across 10,786 participants aged 5 to 89 from 149 countries, 37% agreed or strongly agreed with this statement. Their agreement strongly correlated (r = .65 to .70) with scores on the short version of the Smartphone Addiction Scale (SAS-SV), the most widely used scale in the field. Younger women had the highest scores on both measures and tended to somewhat underestimate their addiction on the single-item scale. Overall, our results suggest that most people have an accurate self-assessment of their problematic smartphone use. This new 1-item scale (SAS-1) can therefore serve as a brief measure of problematic smartphone use when space is limited.

articles.title: Built to Last: Online operant conditioning induces nocebo expectancies resistant to modification in chronic pain patients and pain-free participants
articles.authors: NA; Izabela Łaska; Daryna Rubanets; Joanna Kłosowska; Przemysław Babel; Elżbieta A. Bajcar
articles.abstract: Digital environments increasingly shape health beliefs and treatment expectancies, including negative expectancies that may contribute to nocebo effects. This study examined whether nocebo expectancies can be induced online through operant conditioning, reduced through verbal modeling, and whether these processes differ between chronic pain patients and pain-free participants. Participants were assigned to one of four groups: experimental pain-free, experimental chronic pain, control pain-free, and control chronic pain. They watched videos of a model receiving pain stimuli either with or without a placebo. During the induction phase, participants rated how much pain they expected the model to experience after each stimulus. In the experimental groups, responses indicating higher expected pain in the placebo condition were reinforced by the experimenter, whereas reinforcement was noncontingent in the control groups. During the modification phase, participants observed the model receiving pain stimuli and were shown ratings allegedly provided by other participants, suggesting that others had rated the model’s pain in the placebo condition as lower than in the control condition; in the control groups, these ratings were presented randomly. After each phase and again after a 3-day delay, participants rated expected pain in the placebo and control conditions. Online operant conditioning effectively induced nocebo expectancies, which were not reduced by online verbal modeling and persisted after a 3-day delay. No significant differences emerged between chronic pain and pain-free participants. These findings suggest that brief expert-delivered online feedback may be sufficient to shape nocebo expectancies that are resistant to subsequent modification.

articles.title: A test of seasonal effects on cognition in tournament chess players
articles.authors: Shaheed Azaad; Yoshihisa Kashima; Nick Haslam
articles.abstract: Research has revealed considerable seasonal variation in human behaviour and emotion. However, whether cognitive performance is similarly seasonal remains an open question. While some studies have found a performance advantage in summer compared to winter, others have found either the reverse effect or no effect at all. Because demographics, locations, and cognitive domains of interest vary between studies, it is difficult to determine whether such heterogeneity reflects systematic variation due to study characteristics or whether significant findings are better explained by sampling variance and publication bias. In the present study, we tested for seasonality in 100,113 chess games played by 27,335 players. Not only did we not find a seasonal effect, but we also found no interactions with age, gender, the absolute latitude of the tournament location, and the human development index of a player’s country. Our results suggest that the cognitive capacities required by chess do not vary seasonally, and that there are unlikely to be populations or locations in which it does.

articles.title: Pseudo-Factor Analysis with Item Intercepts
articles.authors: Nigel Guenole
articles.abstract:
We extend the pseudo-factor analysis method by adding a mean structure to obtain pseudo-intercepts. We do so by substituting the
item response mean vector based on human responses with a vector of scalars obtained by projecting item embeddings via dot products
onto semantic intensity axes capturing the difference between low and high scale anchors. Across six scales measuring the trait domains
of the DSM-5 alternative model for personality disorders in occupational settings, the mean correlation between empirical and pseudo-intercepts was r = .54, while the mean correlation between empirical and pseudo-intercepts obtained via deliberate projection onto
randomly incorrect semantic axes was r = −.35. The inclusion of embedding-based mean proxies in pseudo-factor analysis can potentially
enhance scale development and allow mean and covariance structure modeling without empirical data. This may open the possibility of
examining relative item locations, multiple-group pseudo-CFA/SEM modeling, including measurement and structural invariance analyses
across languages and tranformer architectures. Topics such as systematic methods to identify semantic anchors and risks including ‘anchor hacking’ are discussed.

articles.title: A Machine Learning Pipeline to Classify the Type and Severity of Misspecifications in Latent Measurement Models
articles.authors: Melanie Viola Partsch; David Goretzko
articles.abstract: Evaluating and, if necessary, revising factor-analytic latent measurement models is a challenging task. By now, researchers can select from a toolbox of traditional and novel methods to evaluate global and local model fit. However, this toolbox lacks a method to evaluate model fit at an intermediate level facilitating a thoughtful revision process. In this study, we fill this gap with a machine learning (ML) approach to model fit evaluation. Specifically, we introduce an ML pipeline to classify the type and severity of misspecifications in multi-factorial measurement models. To build this pipeline, we trained three ML models that predict a) if the measurement model is correctly specified or if a latent factor, cross-loadings, factor correlations, or residual correlations have been neglected, b) if any, how many cross-loadings have been neglected, and c) if any, how many residual correlations have been neglected. All three ML models performed well overall, with performance drops mainly resulting from misclassifications by ± 1 cross-loading and ± 1–2 residual correlations, respectively. Consequently, the feedback of the ML pipeline must not be implemented in an exact manner. Instead, it points the user to the most likely source of misfit and indicates if the measurement model is not, mildly, moderately, or severely misspecified. This information provides helpful guidance in deciding if and how to revise the measurement model, the measurement instrument, or its underlying theory. We provide an open-access R implementation of the ML pipeline, discuss how to use it within a multi-method approach, and make recommendations on how to use its feedback for the next revision steps.

articles.title: Through Human Eye: Can a Robot Possess a Sense of Self?
articles.authors: Kristina Nikolovska; Jan Pohl; Bernhard Hommel; Arvid Kappas; Francesco Maurelli
articles.abstract: In recent years, there has been significant progress in developing service robots for various settings, prompting a need to examine user--robot interactions. This paper investigates how implementing specific self-characteristics in non-humanoid robots influences user perception. We propose a return to fundamental self-aspects to understand their impact on robot perception. The study examines five fundamental attributes of selfhood (causality, equifinality, behavioral efficiency, learning sensitivity, context sensitivity) and their influence on user perception, utilizing a vehicle-like Duckiebot robot in a controlled experimental setting. By simplifying the complexity of selfhood into manageable traits, we aim to streamline robot development for smoother integration into diverse environments. Our ongoing research aims to contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of human--robot interactions and the development of user-friendly robotic systems that foster positive, trustworthy user experiences.

articles.title: Cultural Worldviews and Environmental Policy Support: Domain-Contingent Associations Across Climate Change and Biodiversity Loss
articles.authors: Aya Takagi; Ryosuke Nakadai
articles.abstract: Social polarisation poses a major challenge to collective climate action. In this context, biodiversity conservation, when aligned with climate objectives, may face less worldview-driven resistance than direct climate policies. However, the links between values of nature and wider cultural worldviews remain poorly understood. We investigated this relationship using a nationwide survey (N = 1,133) in Japan. Individuals with hierarchical worldviews perceived climate change risks as lower and tended to be less supportive of climate change measures than individuals with more egalitarian worldviews. Yet these negative associations were weaker for perceived biodiversity loss risks and for support for climate change-related biodiversity conservation measures, and no direct association was found for stand-alone biodiversity conservation measures. General scientific knowledge was positively associated with support for biodiversity conservation measures, including those related to climate change. Associations between cultural worldviews and policy support differed across climate and biodiversity policy domains.

articles.title: Valence-biased updating differentially influences memory-guided affective ratings.
articles.authors: Ga In Shin; Sarah DuBrow; Vishnu P. Murty
articles.abstract: Understanding how memories of past experiences shape subjective feelings is complicated by the fact that we constantly update our memories. These updates are particularly impactful when individuals are reminded of emotionally positive or negative attributes of the original event. Yet, it remains unclear how such memory updating influences subjective feelings. Here, we investigated how the reactivation of emotional information affects episodic memory, subjective feelings, and their interaction. Across three experiments, participants first learned both positive and negative attributes associated with unfamiliar individuals. Then, they were reminded of a single positive or negative attribute for each individual to reactivate the memory partially. Finally, we reassessed memory for and subjective feelings about each individual’s attributes. In Experiments 1 and 2, these procedures were distributed across three days, while in Experiment 3, they occurred on a single day. Across these three experiments, reminding with negative attributes shifted subjective feelings in a negative direction. Reminded attributes were also better remembered, particularly for negative ones, and changes in subjective feelings were more strongly associated with reminded attributes. However, positive reminders only influenced subjective feelings to change positively when all procedures occurred on the same day. Together, these findings support a model in which memory updating shapes both episodic memory and emotional experience in a valence-dependent manner.

articles.title: From social input to social attention: Parental mood predictability is related to the development of gaze cueing
articles.authors: Dimana V. Atanassova; Marlene Meyer; Sabine Hunnius
articles.abstract:
Gaze following is a fundamental developmental milestone for social cognition. Adults’ attention is reliably cued by gaze shifts but less is known about how gaze cuing emerges in early childhood or what shapes its developmental trajectory. Reduced sensitivity to social input—such as gazes—and differences in the early social environment can affect the development of gaze cueing but the role of these factors remains untested.
We explored the developmental trajectory of the gaze cueing effect (GCE) and the factors shaping it in a large longitudinal sample from the YOUth cohort (N=1928; 5 months, 10 months, 3 years, 6 years). Using static facial stimuli, we found no conclusive evidence of gaze cueing in the first year of life. By 3 years, an early form of the GCE was detectable, with children making more errors on gaze-incongruent trials. By 6 years, the effect was robustly established, yet remained sensitive to characteristics of children’s early social environment.

Contrary to our expectations, reduced social sensitivity indexed by higher callous-unemotional traits did not attenuate the GCE at any age; instead, at 3 years, it was associated with faster overall target detection. Parental mood unpredictability, indexing exposure to an inconsistent social environment, was associated with attenuated gaze cueing at 6 years. In contrast, greater parental mood negativity was associated with slower disengagement from gaze-incongruent targets.

Together, these findings map the developmental trajectory of gaze cueing across early childhood and highlight the role of the parental characteristics as a key factor in shaping early social-cognitive development.

articles.title: Philippe Tissié and Pathological Wanderlust: A Historical Note on the Origin of Exercise Addiction Research
articles.authors: Matthias Weigelt
articles.abstract: Maladaptive and compulsive patterns of exercise behavior – often subsumed under the term exercise addiction – are commonly framed as a proposed form of non substance related behavioral addiction. Research consistently links such patterns to impaired control over exercise, persistent urges to be physically active, and continued engagement despite adverse health and social consequences. The origin of scientific research on exercise addiction is commonly traced back to Baekeland’s seminal study published in 1970. Historical inquiry, however, indicates that scholarly interest in excessive movement behavior emerged much earlier. In particular, the late nineteenth century work of the French physician Philippe Tissié offers an important antecedent. By revisiting Tissié’s descriptions of pathological wanderlust and excessive movement behavior in athletes, this paper positions his observations as a historical precursor to contemporary sport psychological conceptualizations of exercise addiction.

articles.title: When AI Feels like Human: Anthropomorphism, Loneliness, and Problematic Use of Chatbots among School Teachers in Poland
articles.authors: Maciej Ignatowski; Magdalena Duczmal; Marcin Moskalewicz
articles.abstract: The rapid spread of AI chatbots has raised concerns about problematic patterns of use resembling behavioral addictions. This study examined the relationship between problematic AI use, loneliness, and anthropomorphization. Based on a sample of 102 Polish adult AI chatbot users working as school teachers who completed a survey, we found that higher loneliness was associated with stronger anthropomorphization and greater problematic AI use, although the effects were modest. However, the data did not support the mediating role of anthropomorphization. Overall levels of anthropomorphization and social motivation were low, suggesting that these factors may play a limited role in groups that use AI mainly for practical, work-related purposes. Qualitative aspects of engagement may be more informative than time-based indicators alone, highlighting the need to go beyond purely quantitative measures of AI use when assessing problematic engagement.

articles.title: Disinformation in Practice: Threats, Actors, Mechanisms, and Constraints According to Professionals
articles.authors: Grégoire Darcy; Sacha Altay
articles.abstract: How do the professionals charged with fighting disinformation actually understand the problem they are meant to solve? We surveyed 106 French practitioners across military, government, regulatory, and media organizations. They converge on an upstream and offer-driven diagnosis – platforms and amplification structures drive the threat – but describe a field blocked by weak coordination, scarce resources, and fear of censorship accusations. The relationship to science compounds the problem: most practitioners trust peer-reviewed research, yet few engage with it directly, leaving practice disconnected from the evidence base it claims to rely on. Nearly all expect the threat to worsen. Only 13% believe the capacity to respond will catch up.

articles.title: Detecting Response Styles in Visual Analogue Scales
articles.authors: Dominik Vollbracht; Tanja Lischetzke; Mirka Henninger
articles.abstract: Response styles are prevalent in self-report data and may bias parameter estimates. While there are various approaches for detecting response styles in Likert-type data, models for visual analogue scales remain scarce. We propose a novel modeling framework that extends the beta item response model by Noel and Dauvier (2007) to jointly detect extreme response style, midpoint response style, and acquiescent response style. Estimated in a Bayesian framework using Stan, the model performed well in a comprehensive simulation study. Parameter recovery was primarily influenced by the number of (reverse-scored) items and the variance of response styles, whereas sample size had a negligible effect. The model’s practical utility is illustrated using empirical data (Kuhlmann et al., 2017), for which it demonstrated superior model fit compared to the original beta item response model. Overall, the proposed framework provides researchers with a principled approach to account for response styles in visual analogue scale data.

articles.title: What Makes Good Apps Fail or Succeed in the Classroom? The Teacher Support Framework for Integrating Educational Apps (TSF-Apps)
articles.authors: Mirijam Schaaf; Ann-Kathrin Krause; Stephen M. Schueller; Jasmin Breitwieser; Garvin Brod
articles.abstract:
Background: Despite growing access to smartphones and tablets in schools, many educational apps do not succeed in the classroom. Prior research shows that even well-designed and pedagogically grounded tools often fall short when their integration into classroom practice is insufficient. Building on insights from educational psychology and digital health research, we argue that teacher support plays a critical role in determining whether apps become a meaningful part of students’ learning activities. 

Objectives: We propose a new framework, the Teacher Support Framework for Integrating Educational Apps in the Classroom (TSF-Apps), that conceptualizes four key facets where app integration in the classroom can fail: fit, engagement, knowledge, and application. For each facet, we demonstrate how targeted teacher scaffolding can support effective app use and learning. 

Methods: To illustrate its diagnostic and practical value, we apply the framework to a case study of a mobile app designed to foster self-regulated learning in young students. We draw on mixed-methods data (app logs, questionnaire data, and open-ended answers from N = 263 students, Mage = 11.6 years; two teacher interviews) to identify points of failure and corresponding teacher support needs. 

Results and conclusion: The case study demonstrated that the app’s effectiveness was limited by short-lived engagement, shallow understanding of the content, and weak transfer of strategies into everyday learning activities. The findings highlight the need for teacher scaffolding of both technology use and learning content. The proposed framework offers a structured lens for researchers and practitioners seeking to improve the integration of educational apps in classrooms.

articles.title: Translational Literacy Framework (TLF): A Pedagogical Architecture for Cross-Disciplinary Evidence Evaluation and Communication in the AI Era
articles.authors: Caleb Braxton Bragg
articles.abstract: Students graduate from college knowing how to evaluate evidence in their own field but largely unable to evaluate evidence from fields they were not trained in, or to communicate complex findings to audiences who do not share their expertise. This is not a motivation problem. It is a structural one: no course, credential, or framework explicitly teaches this skill. I call this skill translational literacy, the capacity to read, evaluate, and communicate scientific evidence across disciplinary boundaries and across expert-to-public boundaries. This paper introduces the Translational Literacy Framework (TLF), a pedagogical architecture for developing translational literacy at the undergraduate level. The TLF operates through three integrated components: Convergent Triadic Instruction (CTI), in which natural science, social/behavioral science, and library/information science faculty co-deliver instruction in designed convergence; a Modular Disciplinary Architecture (MDA), consisting of portable, discipline-specific instructional modules that can be configured by any institution; and AI Interrogation, a critical stance that positions generative AI as a scientific instrument to be calibrated, pressed, and questioned rather than trusted. I argue that translational literacy is the meta-competency (i.e., a higher-order capacity that integrates and coordinates several lower-order competencies) that connects scientific literacy, information literacy, data literacy, and science communication, adding the cross-disciplinary evaluation layer that none of these provides on its own. The TLF is designed for operationalization through a proposed Certificate in Applied Scientific Communication at Central Connecticut State University, which would be among the first AI-integrated undergraduate scientific communication credentials in U.S. higher education. The framework maps directly onto the Career Readiness Pathway (Bragg & Conway, 2026): the Translational Literacy in Science course is designed to develop career-specific KSAs and core competencies (Pillars 1 and 2), while employer-matched micro-internships provide experiential learning opportunities and career mentorship (Pillars 3 and 4). Empirical validation from a pilot implementation (N = 20, Spring 2027) will be reported separately. The authoritative version of the Translational Literacy Framework (TLF), including licensing terms, is available on the Open Science Framework (OSF) under a Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

articles.title: The unique needs and experiences of autistic students in higher education
articles.authors: Elisabeth Deilhaug; Elna Wennerfeldt Grønnås; Emilie Meyer Bull; Tamara Kalandadze; Liv Merve Abrahamsson; Henri Pesonen; Kjersti Mæhlum Walle; Knut Kolskår; Tonje Torske; Maria Tanaka Johansen
articles.abstract:
Objective
The number of students in higher education with an autism diagnosis has increased steadily over the decades. Acquiring more knowledge about autistic students’ experiences and needs is proposed to help educational institutions promoting and developing supports that may mitigate some of the challenges these students face.

Methods
A web-based survey was used to investigate the accommodation access, experiences and needs of autistic students in Norway, as well as their experiences with the social and academic aspects of student life. To elucidate experiences and needs that are unique to autistic students, data from non-autistic students with and without accommodation needs were collected. Both current students and former students who completed their studies at a Norwegian university or college after 2010 could participate.

Results
In total, the survey garnered 457 responses. Autistic students express a higher need for academic, social and environmental accommodations, face more obstacles socially, report higher rates of loneliness and experiences of stigma, have higher dropout rates and lower satisfaction scores with both accommodations and the student experience. Special interests correlated to the field of study, memory, analytic skills and independent thinking and “hyper focus” was highlighted as strengths among many autistic students in their open-ended responses. Sub-group analyses revealed that autistic students faced more challenges if they had co-occurring diagnoses, late age of diagnosis or identified as not being male.

Conclusion
We found that autistic students report unique experiences and support needs. Higher educational institutions are urged to enhance their support frameworks to better address the needs of autistic students via a more individualised approach, promoting social and academic inclusion and achievement. The accommodations may involve mitigating the challenges autistic students face and simultaneously encouraging both students and staff to recognise and foster the strengths and perspectives of autistic students.

articles.title: Die 4K im Gewand empirischer Forschung: Eine Antwort auf Albrecht et al. (2025)
articles.authors: Benedikt Wisniewski; Tanja Marie Fritz; Martin Daumiller
articles.abstract: Die sogenannten „4K“ (Kommunikation, Kollaboration, kritisches Denken und Kreativität) spielen eine zentrale Rolle in aktuellen bildungspolitischen Diskursen und werden zunehmend empirisch modelliert. Der Beitrag von Albrecht et al. (2025) beansprucht, Struktur und Förderbarkeit dieser Kompetenzen im schulischen Kontext nachzuweisen. Die vorliegende Replik nimmt diesen Beitrag zum Anlass für eine systematische epistemische und methodische Prüfung. Im Zentrum steht die Frage, inwieweit die zugrunde gelegten Konstrukte theoretisch hinreichend bestimmt, empirisch trennbar und belastbar interpretierbar sind. Die Analyse zeigt, dass die berichteten Befunde die angenommene Struktur der 4K nur eingeschränkt stützen und dass die Prä-Post-Daten aufgrund des Designs keine starken Aussagen über Interventionseffekte erlauben. Am Beispiel von Albrecht et al. (2025) wird damit deutlich, welche besonderen Herausforderungen entstehen, wenn normativ etablierte Kom-petenzmodelle in empirische Mess- und Interventionsforschung überführt werden. Wir plädieren entsprechend für eine klarere Trennung zwischen bildungspolitischer Zielsetzung, theoretischer Modellbildung und empirischer Prüfung.

articles.title: Whoever is Not With Me is Against Me: The ‘Moderate as Out-Group’ Effect
articles.authors: Giulia Maimone; Craig McKenzie
articles.abstract:
Common intuition suggests that expressing moderate views would allow people to appeal to the broadest audience possible. But is that really the case? Do moderates please all sides or please no side? Across five preregistered studies (N = 3,272), we show that people holding a partisan view on a sociopolitical issue perceive moderates (i.e., people disclosing a genuinely non-extreme position) as belonging to the out-group ideology. We find that the ‘moderate as out-group’ effect occurs when sociopolitical issues are moralized and at the same time the opposing side is perceived to be a threat to oneself, close ones, or society at large. We also present evidence that the effect is due to partisans’ perception that moderates lack out-group hate, rather than lack in-group love. In other words, partisans perceive that moderates agree with—or simply don’t condemn—the opposing (immoral and threatening) ideology, rather than disagree with their own. Consistent with this, the ‘moderate as out-group’ effect occurs when the moderate view is framed as pro-both sides, but it is attenuated when it is framed as pro-neither side.


©American Psychological Association, 2026. This paper is not the copy of record and may not exactly replicate the authoritative document published in the APA journal. The final article is available, upon publication, at: 10.1037/xge0001957

articles.title: Emotion Regulation Strategies in Adults With ADHD Tendencies: Structural Roles of Reappraisal and Suppression in Relation to Mental Health
articles.authors: Ayako Nakashita
articles.abstract:
Objective: Emotion dysregulation is common in adults with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and is closely related to mental health. This study examined whether reappraisal and suppression partly account for the association between ADHD tendencies and mental health, and whether their structural roles differ according to the level of ADHD tendencies.
Method: A web-based survey was conducted with 294 adults aged 18 years and older. ADHD tendencies were assessed using the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS), emotion regulation strategies using the Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ), and mental health using the K6. Participants were categorized into above- and below-cutoff groups based on ASRS screening scores for multi-group comparison.
Results: Preliminary analyses did not support moderation effects of reappraisal or suppression. Mediation analyses suggested that emotion regulation strategies partly accounted for the association between ADHD tendencies and mental health, particularly through reappraisal. Multi-group structural equation modeling showed that the indirect pathway through reappraisal was relatively consistent across groups, whereas the suppression–mental health path differed significantly between groups and was significant only in the below-cutoff group.
Conclusion: Reappraisal and suppression did not appear to moderate the ADHD–mental health association but were involved in this association as mediating pathways, although their roles differed. The group-specific role of suppression suggests that its association with mental health may vary across levels of ADHD tendencies. These findings indicate that ADHD-related emotion dysregulation may need to be understood not only in terms of habitual strategy use but also in relation to the broader emotional experiences being regulated.

articles.title: Impact of AI on Human Expertise: Canaries in the Coalmine
articles.authors: Andrew J. Waters; Andrea Brancaccio; Shmuel Juni; Fernand Gobet
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence is increasingly able to perform cognitive tasks that were once thought to require human expertise. This raises the question: what happens to human expertise when AI systems equal or exceed expert human performance? We examine this question from the perspective of chess, a domain in which superhuman AI has existed for more than 25 years. We argue that chess provides not a single “canary in the coalmine,” but a set of canaries for how AI may affect human expertise. Chess shows that superhuman AI emerged earlier than expected, developed through multiple approaches, and can operate through mechanisms that share some functional similarities with human expertise. It also recalibrated assumptions about expert judgment, and challenged the expectation that human + AI will always outperform AI alone. Beyond performance, AI has reshaped chess training and knowledge generation, as engines now guide preparation, opaque AI outputs can be translated into human-understandable concepts, and AI-generated ideas can be taught to grandmasters. Finally, chess illustrates institutional consequences of superhuman AI, especially around cheating, governance, and the changing status of human expertise. We consider how these chess-based canaries may generalize to other domains while also emphasizing limits to generalization.

articles.title: Cohort Profile of the INVEST Genetically Informed Full Population Register: Linked Finnish register data, polygenic scores, data opportunities and limitations, and guidance for responsible use in health and social research
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Henrik Dobewall; Outi Sirniö; Charles Ng’iendo; Katri Kantojärvi; Maria Vaalavuo
articles.abstract: We profile the genetically informed part of the INVEST research flagship’s full population register (e17), a Finnish linked data infrastructure that combines rich administrative social and health registers with polygenic scores (PGSs) derived from population health surveys conducted by the Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare (THL). The working paper has three aims. First, it describes the scope and content of the data linkage, including its register modules, the genotyped sample, and sibling-based analytic extensions. Second, it documents the construction of the available PGSs in a format aligned with the Polygenic Risk Score Reporting Statement (Wand et al., 2021, Nature, 591, 211–219). Third, it reviews the data opportunities and limitations and offers guidance for the responsible use of genetically informed designs in health and social research. The INVEST project exploits longitudinal health data, including diagnoses based on the ICD-10 classification recorded in public specialized health care, together with administrative records on social, demographic and economic characteristics of the individuals. With particular attention to dyadic and intergenerational research designs, we highlight the mental health information available across Finnish registers. The genetic component is based on THL’s health surveys, including FINRISK 1992-2012, Health 2000/2011, and FinHealth 2017, and currently includes 39,570 individuals. The paper also outlines weighting strategies developed to partially correct for volunteer bias in the genotyped subsample and describes how partially genotyped sibling designs can be utilized for within-family analyses. In sum, the combined genetically informed register data provide a valuable cohort for sociogenomic research in Finland while also illustrating why careful interpretation and transparent reporting are essential whenever PGSs are used.

articles.title: 50 Years of Collaboration, Author Networks, and Gender Relations: A Longitudinal Comparative Analysis of Board Game Authorship from 1975 to 2025
articles.authors: Johannes Blumenberg
articles.abstract: This study examines gender relations and co-authorship networks in board game design from 1975 to 2025, based on an aggregated BGG dataset (132,062 games; 59,389 designers). Since around 2020, the proportion of female designers has stabilized at 9–10%. Women collaborate significantly more frequently with other women and, on average, work in smaller teams, indicating different network patterns. Bipartite network analyses and linear regression models reveal that higher betweenness centrality, larger teams (with diminishing marginal returns), and greater complexity are associated with higher BGG ratings. In contrast, newcomer teams tend to receive lower ratings. A higher proportion of women in a team is correlated with lower ratings; this relationship has intensified over time and persists even when controlling for complexity. Cohort analyses of the overall network point to a shift in central actors over the decades. Overall, the study underscores the importance of networks in board game authorship and highlights persistent structural inequalities.

articles.title: Trust Me, I Cite Science: How Political Communication Drawing on Science Affects Message Credibility and Communicator Trustworthiness
articles.authors: Daniel Wiesner; Jakob-Moritz Eberl; Andrea Wiesner; Sophie Lecheler
articles.abstract:
The interplay between science and politics is increasingly complex and contentious, especially in times of crises and polarized public debates. Although calls to "follow the science" are often invoked in political discourse, science’s role in politics is rarely straightforward, and it is frequently used strategically beyond its informational function. This study examines how science-related political communication influences perceived message credibility and communicator trustworthiness, integrating insights from science communication and political communication research. 
We empirically test hypotheses across three conceptual dimensions. First, we evaluate the effects of source credibility by comparing communicators (scientists vs. politicians) and their respective uses of evidence. Second, we analyze the impact of different rhetorical strategies employed to communicate the political decision-making process, like highlighting evidence-determined justifications, reasoned processes, or populist appeals. Third, we investigate how science-populist attitudes and pre-existing issue-attitudes moderate these effects. 
Results of a pre-registered factorial survey experiment (N = 552) show that source credibility and the choice of evidence are primary drivers of perceived credibility and trustworthiness. In contrast, rhetorical strategies related to decision-making processes have no significant effect. Notably, science-populist attitudes strongly influence which epistemic authorities respondents consider legitimate bases for political decisions. 
These findings reveal tensions at the science-politics nexus between the strategic effectiveness of constructing epistemic authority and the normative democratic demand for political accountability. The study contributes novel empirical insights into how communicative strategies shape trust in science-related political communication, offering implications for safeguarding trust in science and improving the credibility and trustworthiness of political communication in polarized environments.

articles.title: Time Matters: Rethinking Temporal Dynamics in Criminology
articles.authors: Amy Nivette; Manuel Eisner
articles.abstract: Time is a foundational yet underspecified concept in criminological theory and research. While developmental and life course criminology is explicitly concerned with temporal processes, including the onset, duration, and desistance of criminal behaviour, the precise specification of time in theory and empirical research remains largely implicit. This paper critically examines how time is conceptualized and modelled in criminology, with particular attention to the gap between the temporal assumptions embedded in theory and the designs and methods used to test them. We argue that this underspecification has important consequences for both scientific understanding and practical application, including the design and evaluation of interventions where questions of dosage, timing, and the duration of effects are central. We demonstrate that theoretical and empirical decisions about temporal processes are often driven by convenience or convention rather than explicit reasoning, and that commonly held assumptions about the stability of key constructs over time rest on an insufficient empirical foundation. To address these limitations, we make a case for more formally specifying causal temporal assumptions, integrating measures across micro- and macro-time scales, and adopting research designs, such as ambulatory assessment, experience sampling, and burst designs, capable of capturing how short-term experiences translate into longer-term behavioural change. Greater transparency about temporal assumptions and their empirical basis will strengthen causal inference and improve the design and evaluation of criminological interventions.

articles.title: When Anticorruption Pays off: Voter Support for Anticorruption Efforts in Latin America
articles.authors: Gustavo Guajardo
articles.abstract: While anticorruption appeals are commonly employed by politicians to win votes, the effectiveness of such appeals varies considerably. When do voters reward politicians for anticorruption action? Drawing on two original survey experiments in Colombia and Mexico, this study finds evidence of voters relying on contextual features to evaluate politicians proposing anticorruption policies. The findings underscore how, while voters always value anticorruption action compared to inaction, evaluations are higher in the absence of a recent corruption scandal and when proposed by an opposition politician. While respondents generally prefer punitive policies, the effect was only significant in Colombia.  Remarkably, the preference for punitive policies is shown to be conditional on incumbency for both countries---incumbent party politicians benefit from punitive proposals, while opposition politicians do not. These findings offer new insights into how voters evaluate anticorruption appeals, showing how appeals can boost electoral outcomes, regardless of whether politicians intend to follow through.

articles.title: Algorithmic Reality as a Psychological Ecology: Epistemic Destabilization, Significance Threat, and Regressive Meaning Making in Digital Environments
articles.authors: Mitchell Sanders
articles.abstract:
Human belief formation serves not only epistemic functions but also regulatory ones, helping individuals manage uncertainty, identity coherence, and affective distress (Conway & Pleydell-Pearce, 2000; Kunda, 1990). Contemporary digital environments increasingly mediate these processes through algorithmically curated information streams optimized for engagement rather than epistemic coherence (Praiser, 2011; Gillespie, 2018; Zuboff, 2019). This paper argues that such environments function as psychological ecologies that systematically destabilize epistemic conditions while amplifying motivational and affective pressures toward belief rigidity. Within these ecologies, epistemic destabilization is converted to motivational urgency, and rigid belief adoption functions as a regulatory solution rather than as an epistemic failure.
Drawing on cognitive psychology, moral psychology, and clinical science, the paper advances an amplification model in which chronic uncertainty, narrative saturation, moral salience, and algorithmic reinforcement interact with individual differences in curiosity, intolerance of uncertainty, and identity stability (Kruglanski, 2004; Carleton, 2016; Baird et al., 2012). Under such conditions, belief adoption serves a regulatory function by restoring coherence, significance, and moral orientation, even when epistemic accuracy is compromised. Algorithmic reinforcement dynamics such as repetition, attentional salience, processing fluency, and identity affirming feedback further consolidate these beliefs, increasing resistance to correction and revision (Hasher et al., 1977; Dechene et al., 2010; Pennycook & Rand, 2019).
The framework calls on Significance Quest Theory to explain why epistemic destabilization becomes motivationally urgent for some individuals, highlighting how algorithmic environments can both generate significance threat and supply readily available restoration pathways grounded in moralization, oppositional identity, and narrative certainty (Haidt, 2001; Kruglanski et al., 2014, 2022). These pathways may provide a subjective sense of autonomy while simultaneously constraining reflective agency, creating self reinforcing regulatory loops. Importantly, this work does not claim that digital environments directly cause psychopathology. Instead, it adopts an amplification perspective in which algorithmic contexts intensify latent vulnerabilities ranging from normative cognitive biases to belief rigidity and subclinical disturbance (Bentall et al., 2001; Vorontsova et al., 2013).
By conceptualizing algorithmic media as psychological ecologies, this paper offers a unifying theoretical account linking digital reinforcement structures to belief rigidity, identity fixation, and epistemic closure. The framework delineates scope conditions, specifies underlying psychological mechanisms, and generates testable claims, providing a coherent basis for future empirical research and clinically informed preventative reflection.

articles.title: Mobility measurement and network structure shape mortality inequalities in epidemic simulations
articles.authors: Jordan D. Klein
articles.abstract: Digital trace mobility data are now widely used to infer infectious contacts and parameterize transmission dynamics within epidemiological simulations. However, mobility is not a single observable quantity and may be operationalized in mathematically distinct ways that exhibit different patterns of socioeconomic stratification. Using a metapopulation model applied to COVID-19 in Brazil, we examine how alternative operationalizations of mobility magnitude and network structure affect inferred epidemic mortality inequality dynamics. Specifically, we compare mobility measures based on the number of spatial units visited versus the probability of remaining within a spatial unit, as well as static versus dynamic mobility network formulations derived from multiple data sources, while holding remaining model inputs constant. Across model specifications, socioeconomic inequalities in mortality ultimately emerge, but their apparent timing differs systematically depending on how mobility is represented within the model. Simulations parameterized using mobility measures based on the number of spatial units visited and static mobility networks produced earlier emergence of mortality inequalities than simulations parameterized using stay-put probability measures and dynamic mobility networks. These differences arise because alternative mobility measures capture distinct socially stratified dimensions of mobility and dynamic mobility networks contracted and became more localized and assortative during the early pandemic period. These findings demonstrate that mobility operationalization constitutes a substantively consequential modeling choice capable of altering inferred epidemic inequality trajectories even under otherwise equivalent epidemiological conditions. More broadly, these results demonstrate that representational assumptions embedded within epidemiological models can shape inferred epidemic inequalities, bearing consequences for equity in infectious disease research and response.

articles.title: Americans Favor Predistributive Over Redistributive Economic Policies
articles.authors: David Broska; Jonne Kamphorst; Robb Willer
articles.abstract: Redistributive policies designed to reduce economic inequality through taxes and transfers (e.g., safety-net benefits funded by progressive taxation) receive less support in the U.S. than in many Western democracies. Recent work suggests Americans may be more receptive to “predistributive” policies, which seek to reduce economic inequality before taxes and transfers (e.g., minimum-wage increases). Yet existing evidence leaves open whether this support gap characterizes contemporary Americans overall, and whether it persists when the same policy proposals are described in predistributive versus redistributive terms, with their core provisions held constant. Across 31 nationally representative U.S. survey waves fielded from 2015 to 2024 (total N=384,248), we find greater support for policies classified as predistributive than redistributive. We then fielded a preregistered experiment with a national U.S. sample (N=1,009), randomly varying whether otherwise identical policy proposals were described as increasing earnings for people lower in the income distribution, shifting resources from higher- to lower-income individuals, or presented only with the brief policy description given in all conditions. Support was higher under predistributive than redistributive descriptions. Comparison with the control condition clarifies this difference: predistributive descriptions received support comparable to control, whereas redistributive descriptions received lower support. Exploratory analyses suggest perceived respect for hard work and deservingness of beneficiaries were associated with the support gap. Together, these results suggest Americans prefer policies that reduce inequality by influencing earnings more than through taxes and transfers.

articles.title: Elecciones 2025. Sexto Informe C22-CEP, el agotamiento de la seguridad
articles.authors: Aldo Mascareño; Fabian Belmar; Juan Rozas; Pablo A. Henríquez
articles.abstract: Este sexto informe de la serie C22-CEP sobre las elecciones presidenciales 2025 analiza las visualizaciones y emociones de seis candidatos de cara a las elecciones del 16 de noviembre de 2025: Jeannette Jara, Franco Parisi, Harold Mayne-Nicholls, Evelyn Matthei, José Antonio Kast y Johannes Kaiser. El análisis se basa en un corpus de 901.112 palabras (distribuidas entre 15.160 publicaciones) obtenidas de Facebook e Instagram entre el 1 de agosto y el 30 de septiembre de 2025 y procesadas mediante técnicas de minería de texto y análisis de frecuencias léxicas. Nuestro argumento en este informe es que el tema de seguridad ha agotado su novedad. Este agotamiento de la seguridad como tema de campaña no implica que ya no sea relevante, sino que no produce nueva información para los públicos. Los resultados muestran que el agotamiento de la seguridad tiene varios efectos: 1) un surgimiento disperso de otras propuestas temáticas en algunos casos asociadas a los programas; 2) un aumento de polémicas entre candidatos; 3) un descenso de la primacía que Kast tenía en la derecha hace algunos meses; y 4) la predominancia del enojo como emoción central en los candidatos. La comunicación política transitó desde una configuración centrada exclusivamente en confrontaciones entre candidatos hacia una que incorpora propuestas programáticas. Los debates presidenciales y la publicación de programas electorales en septiembre forzaron este desplazamiento temático, especialmente visible en Matthei, Kaiser y Jara. El artículo concluye que la extensión del período de campaña dificulta cualquier capacidad de generar información innovadora de manera constante. De ahí el agotamiento de la seguridad como tema, así como el incremento de polémicas irrelevantes y la dispersión temática en la comunicación política en torno a los candidatos.

articles.title: Elecciones 2025. Quinto Informe C22-CEP, programas presidenciales
articles.authors: Fabian Belmar; Aldo Mascareño; Juan Rozas; Pablo A. Henríquez
